topic.what: Comprehensive survey and ethnobotanical overview of African traditional medicine natural products used for gastric ulcer treatment, cataloguing plant species, preparation methods, active compounds where known, and pharmacological evidence across multiple African healing traditions.
topic.how: Systematic literature review of African ethnobotanical studies, pharmacological reports, and phytochemical investigations published up to 2022; species organized by geographic region, chemical class, and evidence quality; gaps between traditional use and pharmacological validation mapped.
topic.why_matters: Africa hosts extraordinary botanical biodiversity and diverse healing traditions (West, East, Central, Southern African medicine systems) with largely undocumented anti-ulcer pharmacopoeia; this survey provides the first structured overview bridging ethnobotanical knowledge with experimental evidence, identifying high-priority species for drug discovery.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number and regional distribution of African plants documented for gastric ulcer use
characteristics.value: Dozens of species from multiple African regions documented; broad chemical diversity (tannins, alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, terpenoids)
characteristics.vs_baseline: Prior reviews covered <10 species per subregion
characteristics.evidence: Systematic literature compilation across African ethnobotanical databases and primary studies
characteristics.context: Survey quality depends on publication bias toward English-language and indexed African research

characteristics.what_it_measures: Proportion of documented plants with pharmacological validation vs traditional use only
characteristics.value: Majority of documented plants have only ethnobotanical evidence; minority have in-vitro or in-vivo pharmacological studies
characteristics.context: Highlights large discovery gap between traditional knowledge and scientific validation

characteristics.what_it_measures: Range of phytochemical classes represented in anti-ulcer African plants
characteristics.value: Tannins, alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, phenolic acids, terpenoids — broader than single-tradition pharmacopoeias
characteristics.vs_baseline: European ethnopharmacology focuses heavily on flavonoids and terpenoids
characteristics.evidence: Phytochemical reports for characterized active species
characteristics.context: Diverse chemical space suggests potential for novel mechanisms and scaffolds

good_for: Identifying high-priority African species for anti-gastric-ulcer drug discovery
Systematic ethnobotanical documentation of understudied African healing systems
Comparative cross-cultural analysis of anti-ulcer plant pharmacology
Guiding phytochemical isolation and bioactivity studies in African flora
not_for: Direct phytochemical or mechanism data (survey compilation, not primary research)
Regions outside Africa (different species and traditions)
requires: In-vivo validation studies for priority species identified in survey
Ethnobotanical fieldwork to document preparation methods and dosing accurately
Anti-H. pylori screening for documented species (ulcer etiology often H. pylori-linked)

novelty: Most comprehensive English-language survey of African traditional medicine for gastric ulcer to date at time of publication
Maps the geographic and chemical diversity of African anti-ulcer flora across multiple traditions for the first time in a unified framework
Identifies critical evidence gaps, prioritizing which African species deserve urgent pharmacological investigation
open_problems: Most documented species lack even basic in-vitro pharmacological validation
Traditional dosing, preparation, and combination regimens poorly documented in scientific literature
Anti-H. pylori activity of most listed species has not been tested
Publication bias likely underrepresents Central African, North African, and non-anglophone traditions
Access and intellectual property issues around commercializing African traditional knowledge not addressed
